The [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League "1999–2000 Football League") was [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.")'s 93rd season in [the Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") and sixth in the [third tier](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division").

For the third pre-season Stoke were looking for a new manager but the directors were finding it difficult with first choice [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis") joining [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") openly admitting that he felt the board lacked ambition. Second choice was [Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson") and he accepted the job offer as did [John Rudge](/wiki/John_Rudge "John Rudge") who became director of football. Stoke slowly built up some decent results but there were still tensions between fans and the board and so when news spread that an Icelandic business consortium wanted to take over it became a no-brainer. Unfortunately for Megson new chairman [Gunnar Gíslason](/wiki/Gunnar_G%C3%ADslason_(businessman) "Gunnar Gíslason (businessman)") appointed his own man, former Iceland national manager [Gudjon Thordarson](/wiki/Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson "Guðjón Þórðarson"). In came a number of Icelandic players and the side did well winning the [Football League Trophy](/wiki/2000_Football_League_Trophy_Final "2000 Football League Trophy Final") and reached the play-offs against [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C."). After beating the "Gills" 3–2 in the first leg, two controversial refereeing decisions cost Stoke dearly and they lost the second leg 3–0.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1)

## Season review

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Season review")]

### League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: League")]

For the third consecutive summer, Stoke were hunting for a new manager, this time they had a clear favourite, [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) However Pulis decided to join [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") after openly admitting that he felt the board lacked ambition.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) Second choice was [Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson"), hard working and honest manager in the same mould as Pulis and he accepted Stoke's job offer.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) He appointed [Nigel Pearson](/wiki/Nigel_Pearson "Nigel Pearson") as his assistant and surprisingly [John Rudge](/wiki/John_Rudge "John Rudge") was made [director of football](/wiki/Director_of_football "Director of football") after spending a long time at [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) There was little expectation as the season began with some steady if unspectacular results and by the end of August [Lárus Sigurðsson](/wiki/L%C3%A1rus_Sigur%C3%B0sson "Lárus Sigurðsson") was sold to [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C.") for £350,000.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") was proving to be a top player in the Second Division he scored the first few of his 30 goals.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) Stoke then went on a nine match unbeaten run and lifted themselves into the play-offs.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) In October news spread that a group of Icelandic businessmen were interested in taking control of the club and by mid November Stoke fans had their wishes granted and [Gunnar Gíslason](/wiki/Gunnar_G%C3%ADslason_(businessman) "Gunnar Gíslason (businessman)") completed the Icelandic takeover under the name of [Stoke Holding](/wiki/Stoke_Holding "Stoke Holding").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1)

[Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson") through no fault of his own was replaced by [Gudjon Thordarson](/wiki/Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson "Guðjón Þórðarson") and in came a number of Icelandic players and the new era began well with Stoke beating [Wycombe Wanderers](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") 4–0 away.[[2]](#cite_note-2) There was now a much better feeling about the club with the injection of new money and players but this was knocked back greatly in early 2000.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) On 23 February 2000 arguably one of the greatest footballers the world has ever seen [Stanley Matthews](/wiki/Stanley_Matthews "Stanley Matthews") died at the age of 85. Matthews was not just a club legend but also to [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") as a whole.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) He was remembered in a perfect way with Stoke beating [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C.") 5–1 with Thorne scoring four goals.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Stoke maintained their form from this match and went unbeaten until the final match of the season but despite a 1–0 defeat at [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") Stoke finished two points above [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") and entered the play-offs.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) Their opponents were [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.") and in the first leg 22,124 saw Stoke make a great start going into a 2–0 lead thanks to [Arnar Gunnlaugsson](/wiki/Arnar_Gunnlaugsson "Arnar Gunnlaugsson") and [Kyle Lightbourne](/wiki/Kyle_Lightbourne "Kyle Lightbourne") Gillingham pulled one back but Thorne restored Stoke's two goal advantage. [Andy Hessenthaler](/wiki/Andy_Hessenthaler "Andy Hessenthaler") then scored in injury time meaning the second leg would be a very tough match.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) In the second leg at [Priestfield Stadium](/wiki/Priestfield_Stadium "Priestfield Stadium") referee [Rob Styles](/wiki/Rob_Styles "Rob Styles") sent off both [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") and [Clive Clarke](/wiki/Clive_Clarke "Clive Clarke") for minor offences and Gillingham went on to win 3–0.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1)

### FA Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: FA Cup")]

Another early exit for Stoke this season, defeated 2–0 away at [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1)

### League Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: League Cup")]

After beating last seasons opponents [Macclesfield Town](/wiki/Macclesfield_Town_F.C. "Macclesfield Town F.C.") Stoke lost 3–1 to Premier League [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1)

### League Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1999%E2%80%932000_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: League Trophy")]

Stoke began their [Football League Trophy](/wiki/Football_League_Trophy "Football League Trophy") campaign on a cold December night against Third Division [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington_F.C. "Darlington F.C.") at the Britannia Stadium. With the scores level after normal time [Kyle Lightbourne](/wiki/Kyle_Lightbourne "Kyle Lightbourne") scored a golden goal to send Stoke through.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) Away wins over [Oldham Athletic](/wiki/Oldham_Athletic_A.F.C. "Oldham Athletic A.F.C."), [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") and [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C.") saw Stoke in the area final against [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_A.F.C. "Rochdale A.F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) A 3–1 win in the first leg was followed by a 1–0 win in the second earning a place in the final against [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1) A crowd of 75,057 at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") saw [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") opened the scoring for Stoke before [Paul Holland](/wiki/Paul_Holland_(footballer) "Paul Holland (footballer)") equalised for the Robins. [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") scored a close range winner for Stoke after 82 minutes earning Stoke their second Football League Trophy win.[[1]](#cite_note-Stoke_City_Modern_Era-1)
